[http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] {{refend}} * {{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |location=Tokyo |year=2000 |edition=First Edition |chapter=The Birth of the Japanese nation in |isbn=4-333-01684-3 |page=232 pages }} ==External links== *[http://www.oomiwa.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) {{commoncat|Ohmiwa-jinja|Ōmiwa Shrine}} {{coord|34|31|44|N|135|51|10|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Omiwa Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[ja:大神神社]]

{{about|the shrine in [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai city]]|similarly named shrines|Ōmiwa Shrine (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Ōmiwa Shrine
大神神社 | image = Ohmiwa Shrine Big Torii.jpg | caption = Large ''[[torii]]'' in front of Mount Miwa | type = | dedication = [[Ōmononushi]]
[[Ōnamuchi]]
[[Sukunahikona-no-kami]]
[[Mount Miwa]] | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 1422 Miwa, [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai-shi]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara-ken]] | phone = 0744-42-6633 | website = http://www.oomiwa.or.jp/ }} {{nihongo|'''Ōmiwa Shrine'''|大神神社|Ōmiwa-jinja}}, also known as {{nihongo|'''Miwa Shrine'''|三輪神社|Miwa-jinja|alternately written as 三輪明神 ''Miwa-myōjin''}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai]], [[Nara prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]].Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 252-286. The shrine is noted because it contains no sacred images or objects because it is believed to serve [[Mount Miwa]], the mountain on which it stands.{{en icon}} [http://www.oomiwa.or.jp/eng.html Ōmiwa Shrine site] For the same reason, it has a {{nihongo|worship hall|拝殿|''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]''}}, but no {{nihongo|place for the deity to be housed|神殿|''shinden''}}. In this sense, it is a model of what the first Shinto shrines were like.Tamura, page 21 Ōmiwa Shrine is one of the oldest extant Shinto shrines in Japan and the site has been sacred ground for some of the [[Ko-shinto (Jomon)|earliest religious practices]] in Japan. Because of this, it has sometimes been named as Japan's first shrine. Ōmiwa Shrine is a tutelary shrine of the Japanese [[sake]] brewers. ==History== Ōmiwa Shrine's history is closely related to Mount Miwa and the religious practices surrounding the mountain. In the early [[Kofun period]], Yamato kings and leaders had shifted their attention to ''[[kami]]'' worship on Mount Miwa, and Ōmiwa Shrine was the major institution for this branch of worship.Brown (1993), 116-117. The style of Shinto surrounding Miwa became later known as [[Miwa Shinto]], and is set apart from previous practices by a more structured theological philosophy. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines, including the Ōmiwa Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Ōmiwa Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank among government supported shrines. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Religious significance== {{main|Mount Miwa}} The Ōmiwa Shrine is directly linked to Mount Miwa in that the mountain is the shrine's ''[[shintai]]'', or "kami-body", instead of a building housing a "kami-body". This type of mountain worship (''[[shintai-zan]]'') is found in the earliest forms of [[Shinto]], and has also been employed at [[Suwa Shrine]] in [[Nagano Prefecture|Nagano]], and formerly at [[Isonokami Shrine]] in [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] and [[Munakata Shrine]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]]. According to the chronicle ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', [[Emperor Sujin]] appealed to Mount Miwa's [[kami]] when Japan was crippled by [[Plague (disease)|plague]]. In response, the kami Ōmononushi demanded rituals be performed for him at Mount Miwa. He then demanded that the rites be led by [[Ōtata Neko]], his half-kami, half-human son born from the union with a woman of the [[Miwa clan]]. Ōtata Neko performed the rites to satisfaction, and the plague subsided. A building dedicated to Ōtata Neko was later erected in his honor. A legendary white snake is said to live in around the shrine, and is supposedly one of the kami worshipped there. Indeed, snakes and the snake cult figures importantly in the myths surrounding Mount Miwa as well as early Shinto in general. ==Architecture== [[Image:Ohmiwa Shrine 2nd Torii.jpg|thumb|170px|left|Second ''torii'' leading to the inner sanctuary]] Ōmiwa Shrine is situated in a quiet forest, and built directly in front of Mount Miwa. An ancient [[Japanese cedar]] tree (''Cryptomeria'') can be found on shrine compound, and is considered sacred. The mountain itself serves as the ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, instead of a man-made building. [[Image:AnneauxBorromeensFace.png|thumb|100px|right|Borromean rings motif]] Decorations in the form of [[Borromean rings]] are found throughout the shrine's buildings. This ornamentation symbolizes the three rings, as "Miwa" is written with the [[kanji]] for "three" (三) and "ring" (輪). Built in 1984, at 32 m the ''torii'' on its [[sandō]] is the second highest in Japan.{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/an/Bauten:Bekannte_Schreine|title= Bekannte Schreine - Religion-in-Japan|last=Scheid|first=Bernhard|publisher=University of Vienna|language=German|accessdate=20 September 2010}} The shrine also has a great ''shime torii'', an ancient form of gate made only with two posts and a rope called ''[[shimenawa]]''. It is one of few shrines that has a "triple-''torii''" (''[[miwa torii]]'') on its grounds. This gate is also one of the few to actually have doors, which bar access to the mountain it enshrines. The buildings at Ōmiwa Shrine are a mix of structures built from ancient times to the [[Edo period]]. ==National treasures== ===Important Cultural Assets=== *The entire shrine compound *The 17th century ''haiden'', or prayer hall, built with cypress [[bark]] roofing *The "Triple-''torii''" (''miwa torii'') *The ''shinden'' dedicated to [[Ōtata Neko]]. *Suit of bronze armor, lacquered red *A copy of the ''[[Book of Zhou]]'', scroll number 19. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Mount Miwa]] * [[Ko-shinto (Jomon)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[William George Aston|Aston]], William George (2005). ''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697''. Boston: [[Tuttle Publishing]]. ISBN 0804836744. * Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82363-4 * Brown, Delmer M. (1993). ''Cambridge History of Japan'', Volume 1. New York: [[Cambridge University Press]]. ISBN 0521223520. * Kidder, Jonathan Edward (2007). ''Himiko and Japan's elusive chiefdom of Yamatai: archaeology, history, and mythology''. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. ISBN 0824830350. * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [http://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&dq=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&client=firefox-a ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] {{refend}} * {{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |location=Tokyo |year=2000 |edition=First Edition |chapter=The Birth of the Japanese nation in |isbn=4-333-01684-3 |page=232 pages }} ==External links== *[http://www.oomiwa.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) {{commoncat|Ohmiwa-jinja|Ōmiwa Shrine}} {{coord|34|31|44|N|135|51|10|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Omiwa Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[ja:大神神社]]

The mountain itself serves as the ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, instead of a man-made building. [[Image:AnneauxBorromeensFace.png|thumb|100px|right|Borromean rings motif]] Decorations in the form of [[Borromean rings]] are found throughout the shrine's buildings. This ornamentation symbolizes the three rings, as "Miwa" is written with the [[kanji]] for "three" (三) and "ring" (輪). Built in 1984, at 32 m the ''torii'' on its [[sandō]] is the second highest in Japan.{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/an/Bauten:Bekannte_Schreine|title= Bekannte Schreine - Religion-in-Japan|last=Scheid|first=Bernhard|publisher=University of Vienna|language=German|accessdate=20 September 2010}} The shrine also has a great ''shime torii'', an ancient form of gate made only with two posts and a rope called ''[[shimenawa]]''. It is one of few shrines that has a "triple-''torii''" (''[[miwa torii]]'') on its grounds. This gate is also one of the few to actually have doors, which bar access to the mountain it enshrines. The buildings at Ōmiwa Shrine are a mix of structures built from ancient times to the [[Edo period]]. ==National treasures== ===Important Cultural Assets=== *The entire shrine compound *The 17th century ''haiden'', or prayer hall, built with cypress [[bark]] roofing *The "Triple-''torii''" (''miwa torii'') *The ''shinden'' dedicated to [[Ōtata Neko]]. *Suit of bronze armor, lacquered red *A copy of the ''[[Book of Zhou]]'', scroll number 19. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Mount Miwa]] * [[Ko-shinto (Jomon)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[William George Aston|Aston, William George]]. {{about|the shrine in [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai city]]|similarly named shrines|Ōmiwa Shrine (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Ōmiwa Shrine
大神神社 | image = Ohmiwa Shrine Big Torii.jpg | caption = Large ''[[torii]]'' in front of Mount Miwa | type = | dedication = [[Ōmononushi]]
[[Ōnamuchi]]
[[Sukunahikona-no-kami]]
[[Mount Miwa]] | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 1422 Miwa, [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai-shi]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara-ken]] | phone = 0744-42-6633 | website = http://www.oomiwa.or.jp/ }} {{nihongo|'''Ōmiwa Shrine'''|大神神社|Ōmiwa-jinja}}, also known as {{nihongo|'''Miwa Shrine'''|三輪神社|Miwa-jinja|alternately written as 三輪明神 ''Miwa-myōjin''}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai]], [[Nara prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]].Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 252-286. The shrine is noted because it contains no sacred images or objects because it is believed to serve [[Mount Miwa]], the mountain on which it stands.{{en icon}} [http://www.oomiwa.or.jp/eng.html Ōmiwa Shrine site] For the same reason, it has a {{nihongo|worship hall|拝殿|''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]''}}, but no {{nihongo|place for the deity to be housed|神殿|''shinden''}}. In this sense, it is a model of what the first Shinto shrines were like.Tamura, page 21 Ōmiwa Shrine is one of the oldest extant Shinto shrines in Japan and the site has been sacred ground for some of the [[Ko-shinto (Jomon)|earliest religious practices]] in Japan. Because of this, it has sometimes been named as Japan's first shrine. Ōmiwa Shrine is a tutelary shrine of the Japanese [[sake]] brewers. ==History== Ōmiwa Shrine's history is closely related to Mount Miwa and the religious practices surrounding the mountain. In the early [[Kofun period]], Yamato kings and leaders had shifted their attention to ''[[kami]]'' worship on Mount Miwa, and Ōmiwa Shrine was the major institution for this branch of worship.Brown (1993), 116-117. The style of Shinto surrounding Miwa became later known as [[Miwa Shinto]], and is set apart from previous practices by a more structured theological philosophy. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines, including Ōmiwa.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. Ōmiwa shrine was designated as one of two chief Shinto shrines (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Yamato province]]. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, the Ōmiwa Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank among government supported shrines. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Religious significance== {{main|Mount Miwa}} The Ōmiwa Shrine is directly linked to Mount Miwa in that the mountain is the shrine's ''[[shintai]]'', or "kami-body", instead of a building housing a "kami-body". This type of mountain worship (''[[shintai-zan]]'') is found in the earliest forms of [[Shinto]], and has also been employed at [[Suwa Shrine]] in [[Nagano Prefecture|Nagano]], and formerly at [[Isonokami Shrine]] in [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] and [[Munakata Shrine]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]]. According to the chronicle ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', [[Emperor Sujin]] appealed to Mount Miwa's [[kami]] when Japan was crippled by [[Plague (disease)|plague]]. In response, the kami Ōmononushi demanded rituals be performed for him at Mount Miwa. He then demanded that the rites be led by [[Ōtata Neko]], his half-kami, half-human son born from the union with a woman of the [[Miwa clan]]. Ōtata Neko performed the rites to satisfaction, and the plague subsided. A building dedicated to Ōtata Neko was later erected in his honor. A legendary white snake is said to live in around the shrine, and is supposedly one of the kami worshipped there. Indeed, snakes and the snake cult figures importantly in the myths surrounding Mount Miwa as well as early Shinto in general. ==Architecture== [[Image:Ohmiwa Shrine 2nd Torii.jpg|thumb|170px|left|Second ''torii'' leading to the inner sanctuary]] Ōmiwa Shrine is situated in a quiet forest, and built directly in front of Mount Miwa. An ancient [[Japanese cedar]] tree (''Cryptomeria'') can be found on shrine compound, and is considered sacred. The mountain itself serves as the ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, instead of a man-made building. [[Image:AnneauxBorromeensFace.png|thumb|100px|right|Borromean rings motif]] Decorations in the form of [[Borromean rings]] are found throughout the shrine's buildings. This ornamentation symbolizes the three rings, as "Miwa" is written with the [[kanji]] for "three" (三) and "ring" (輪). Built in 1984, at 32 m the ''torii'' on its [[sandō]] is the second highest in Japan.{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/an/Bauten:Bekannte_Schreine|title= Bekannte Schreine - Religion-in-Japan|last=Scheid|first=Bernhard|publisher=University of Vienna|language=German|accessdate=20 September 2010}} The shrine also has a great ''shime torii'', an ancient form of gate made only with two posts and a rope called ''[[shimenawa]]''. It is one of few shrines that has a "triple-''torii''" (''[[miwa torii]]'') on its grounds. This gate is also one of the few to actually have doors, which bar access to the mountain it enshrines. The buildings at Ōmiwa Shrine are a mix of structures built from ancient times to the [[Edo period]]. ==National treasures== ===Important Cultural Assets=== *The entire shrine compound *The 17th century ''haiden'', or prayer hall, built with cypress [[bark]] roofing *The "Triple-''torii''" (''miwa torii'') *The ''shinden'' dedicated to [[Ōtata Neko]]. *Suit of bronze armor, lacquered red *A copy of the ''[[Book of Zhou]]'', scroll number 19. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Mount Miwa]] * [[Ko-shinto (Jomon)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[William George Aston|Aston, William George]]. (2005). ''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697''. Boston: [[Tuttle Publishing]]. ISBN 0804836744. * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82363-4 * Brown, Delmer M. (1993). ''Cambridge History of Japan'', Volume 1. New York: [[Cambridge University Press]]. ISBN 0521223520. * Kidder, Jonathan Edward (2007). ''Himiko and Japan's elusive chiefdom of Yamatai: archaeology, history, and mythology''. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. ISBN 0824830350. * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [http://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&dq=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&client=firefox-a ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] * {{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |location=Tokyo |year=2000 |chapter=The Birth of the Japanese Nation |isbn=4-333-01684-3 }} {{refend}} ==External links== *[http://www.oomiwa.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) {{commoncat|Ohmiwa-jinja|Ōmiwa Shrine}} {{coord|34|31|44|N|135|51|10|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Omiwa Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[ja:大神神社]]

[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, the Ōmiwa Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank among government supported shrines. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Religious significance== {{main|Mount Miwa}} The Ōmiwa Shrine is directly linked to Mount Miwa in that the mountain is the shrine's ''[[shintai]]'', or "kami-body", instead of a building housing a "kami-body". This type of mountain worship (''[[shintai-zan]]'') is found in the earliest forms of [[Shinto]], and has also been employed at [[Suwa Shrine]] in [[Nagano Prefecture|Nagano]], and formerly at [[Isonokami Shrine]] in [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] and [[Munakata Shrine]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]]. According to the chronicle ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', [[Emperor Sujin]] appealed to Mount Miwa's [[kami]] when Japan was crippled by [[Plague (disease)|plague]]. In response, the kami Ōmononushi demanded rituals be performed for him at Mount Miwa. He then demanded that the rites be led by [[Ōtata Neko]], his half-kami, half-human son born from the union with a woman of the [[Miwa clan]]. Ōtata Neko performed the rites to satisfaction, and the plague subsided. A building dedicated to Ōtata Neko was later erected in his honor. A legendary white snake is said to live in around the shrine, and is supposedly one of the kami worshipped there. Indeed, snakes and the snake cult figures importantly in the myths surrounding Mount Miwa as well as early Shinto in general. ==Architecture== [[Image:Ohmiwa Shrine 2nd Torii.jpg|thumb|170px|left|Second ''torii'' leading to the inner sanctuary]] Ōmiwa Shrine is situated in a quiet forest, and built directly in front of Mount Miwa. An ancient [[Japanese cedar]] tree (''Cryptomeria'') can be found on shrine compound, and is considered sacred. The mountain itself serves as the ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, instead of a man-made building. [[Image:AnneauxBorromeensFace.png|thumb|100px|right|Borromean rings motif]] Decorations in the form of [[Borromean rings]] are found throughout the shrine's buildings. This ornamentation symbolizes the three rings, as "Miwa" is written with the [[kanji]] for "three" (三) and "ring" (輪). Built in 1984, at 32 m the ''torii'' on its [[sandō]] is the second highest in Japan.{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/an/Bauten:Bekannte_Schreine|title= Bekannte Schreine - Religion-in-Japan|last=Scheid|first=Bernhard|publisher=University of Vienna|language=German|accessdate=20 September 2010}} The shrine also has a great ''shime torii'', an ancient form of gate made only with two posts and a rope called ''[[shimenawa]]''. It is one of few shrines that has a "triple-''torii''" (''[[miwa torii]]'') on its grounds. This gate is also one of the few to actually have doors, which bar access to the mountain it enshrines. The buildings at Ōmiwa Shrine are a mix of structures built from ancient times to the [[Edo period]]. ==National treasures== ===Important Cultural Assets=== *The entire shrine compound *The 17th century ''haiden'', or prayer hall, built with cypress [[bark]] roofing *The "Triple-''torii''" (''miwa torii'') *The ''shinden'' dedicated to [[Ōtata Neko]]. *Suit of bronze armor, lacquered red *A copy of the ''[[Book of Zhou]]'', scroll number 19. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Mount Miwa]] * [[Ko-shinto (Jomon)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[William George Aston|Aston, William George]]. (2005). ''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697''. Boston: [[Tuttle Publishing]]. ISBN 0804836744. * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82363-4 * Brown, Delmer M. (1993). ''Cambridge History of Japan'', Volume 1. New York: [[Cambridge University Press]]. ISBN 0521223520. * Kidder, Jonathan Edward (2007). ''Himiko and Japan's elusive chiefdom of Yamatai: archaeology, history, and mythology''. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. ISBN 0824830350. * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [http://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&dq=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&client=firefox-a ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] * {{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |location=Tokyo |year=2000 |chapter=The Birth of the Japanese Nation |isbn=4-333-01684-3 }} {{refend}} ==External links== {{commoncat|Ohmiwa-jinja|Ōmiwa Shrine}} *[http://www.oomiwa.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) {{coord|34|31|44|N|135|51|10|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Omiwa Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[ja:大神神社]]

{{about|the shrine in [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai city]]|similarly named shrines|Ōmiwa Shrine (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Ōmiwa Shrine
大神神社 | image = Ohmiwa Shrine Big Torii.jpg | caption = Large ''[[torii]]'' in front of Mount Miwa | type = | dedication = [[Ōmononushi]]
[[Ōnamuchi]]
[[Sukunahikona-no-kami]]
[[Mount Miwa]] | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 1422 Miwa, [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai-shi]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara-ken]] | phone = 0744-42-6633 | website = http://www.oomiwa.or.jp/ }} {{nihongo|'''Ōmiwa Shrine'''|大神神社|Ōmiwa-jinja}}, also known as {{nihongo|'''Miwa Shrine'''|三輪神社|Miwa-jinja|alternately written as 三輪明神 ''Miwa-myōjin''}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai]], [[Nara prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]].Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 252-286. The shrine is noted because it contains no sacred images or objects because it is believed to serve [[Mount Miwa]], the mountain on which it stands.{{en icon}} [http://www.oomiwa.or.jp/eng.html Ōmiwa Shrine site] For the same reason, it has a {{nihongo|worship hall|拝殿|''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]''}}, but no {{nihongo|place for the deity to be housed|神殿|''shinden''}}. In this sense, it is a model of what the first Shinto shrines were like.Tamura, page 21 Ōmiwa Shrine is one of the oldest extant Shinto shrines in Japan and the site has been sacred ground for some of the [[Ko-shinto (Jomon)|earliest religious practices]] in Japan. Because of this, it has sometimes been named as Japan's first shrine. Ōmiwa Shrine is a tutelary shrine of the Japanese [[sake]] brewers. ==History== Ōmiwa Shrine's history is closely related to Mount Miwa and the religious practices surrounding the mountain. In the early [[Kofun period]], Yamato kings and leaders had shifted their attention to ''[[kami]]'' worship on Mount Miwa, and Ōmiwa Shrine was the major institution for this branch of worship.Brown (1993), 116-117. The style of Shinto surrounding Miwa became later known as [[Miwa Shinto]], and is set apart from previous practices by a more structured theological philosophy. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines, including Ōmiwa.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. Ōmiwa was designated as the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Yamato province]]. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, the Ōmiwa Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank among government supported shrines. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Religious significance== {{main|Mount Miwa}} The Ōmiwa Shrine is directly linked to Mount Miwa in that the mountain is the shrine's ''[[shintai]]'', or "kami-body", instead of a building housing a "kami-body". This type of mountain worship (''[[shintai-zan]]'') is found in the earliest forms of [[Shinto]], and has also been employed at [[Suwa Shrine]] in [[Nagano Prefecture|Nagano]], and formerly at [[Isonokami Shrine]] in [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] and [[Munakata Shrine]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]]. According to the chronicle ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', [[Emperor Sujin]] appealed to Mount Miwa's [[kami]] when Japan was crippled by [[Plague (disease)|plague]]. In response, the kami Ōmononushi demanded rituals be performed for him at Mount Miwa. He then demanded that the rites be led by [[Ōtata Neko]], his half-kami, half-human son born from the union with a woman of the [[Miwa clan]]. Ōtata Neko performed the rites to satisfaction, and the plague subsided. A building dedicated to Ōtata Neko was later erected in his honor. A legendary white snake is said to live in around the shrine, and is supposedly one of the kami worshipped there. Indeed, snakes and the snake cult figures importantly in the myths surrounding Mount Miwa as well as early Shinto in general. ==Architecture== [[Image:Ohmiwa Shrine 2nd Torii.jpg|thumb|170px|left|Second ''torii'' leading to the inner sanctuary]] Ōmiwa Shrine is situated in a quiet forest, and built directly in front of Mount Miwa. An ancient [[Japanese cedar]] tree (''Cryptomeria'') can be found on shrine compound, and is considered sacred. The mountain itself serves as the ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, instead of a man-made building. [[Image:AnneauxBorromeensFace.png|thumb|100px|right|Borromean rings motif]] Decorations in the form of [[Borromean rings]] are found throughout the shrine's buildings. This ornamentation symbolizes the three rings, as "Miwa" is written with the [[kanji]] for "three" (三) and "ring" (輪). Built in 1984, at 32 m the ''torii'' on its [[sandō]] is the second highest in Japan.{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/an/Bauten:Bekannte_Schreine|title= Bekannte Schreine - Religion-in-Japan|last=Scheid|first=Bernhard|publisher=University of Vienna|language=German|accessdate=20 September 2010}} The shrine also has a great ''shime torii'', an ancient form of gate made only with two posts and a rope called ''[[shimenawa]]''. It is one of few shrines that has a "triple-''torii''" (''[[miwa torii]]'') on its grounds. This gate is also one of the few to actually have doors, which bar access to the mountain it enshrines. The buildings at Ōmiwa Shrine are a mix of structures built from ancient times to the [[Edo period]]. ==National treasures== ===Important Cultural Assets=== *The entire shrine compound *The 17th century ''haiden'', or prayer hall, built with cypress [[bark]] roofing *The "Triple-''torii''" (''miwa torii'') *The ''shinden'' dedicated to [[Ōtata Neko]]. *Suit of bronze armor, lacquered red *A copy of the ''[[Book of Zhou]]'', scroll number 19. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Mount Miwa]] * [[Ko-shinto (Jomon)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[William George Aston|Aston, William George]]. (2005). ''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697''. Boston: [[Tuttle Publishing]]. ISBN 0804836744. * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82363-4 * Brown, Delmer M. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] * {{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |location=Tokyo |year=2000 |chapter=The Birth of the Japanese Nation |isbn=4-333-01684-3 }} {{refend}} ==External links== {{commoncat|Ohmiwa-jinja|Ōmiwa Shrine}} *[http://www.oomiwa.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) {{coord|34|31|44|N|135|51|10|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Omiwa Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[ja:大神神社]]

The shrine is noted because it contains no sacred images or objects because it is believed to serve [[Mount Miwa]], the mountain on which it stands.{{en icon}} [http://www.oomiwa.or.jp/eng.html Ōmiwa Shrine site] For the same reason, it has a {{nihongo|worship hall|拝殿|''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]''}}, but no {{nihongo|place for the deity to be housed|神殿|''shinden''}}. In this sense, it is a model of what the first Shinto shrines were like.Tamura, page 21 Ōmiwa Shrine is one of the oldest extant Shinto shrines in Japan and the site has been sacred ground for some of the [[Ko-shinto (Jomon)|earliest religious practices]] in Japan. Because of this, it has sometimes been named as Japan's first shrine. Ōmiwa Shrine is a tutelary shrine of the Japanese [[sake]] brewers. ==History== Ōmiwa Shrine's history is closely related to Mount Miwa and the religious practices surrounding the mountain. In the early [[Kofun period]], Yamato kings and leaders had shifted their attention to ''[[kami]]'' worship on Mount Miwa, and Ōmiwa Shrine was the major institution for this branch of worship.Brown (1993), 116-117. The style of Shinto surrounding Miwa became later known as [[Miwa Shinto]], and is set apart from previous practices by a more structured theological philosophy. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines, including Ōmiwa.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. Ōmiwa was designated as the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Yamato province]]. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Ōmiwa was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank among government supported shrines. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Religious significance== {{main|Mount Miwa}} The Ōmiwa Shrine is directly linked to Mount Miwa in that the mountain is the shrine's ''[[shintai]]'', or "kami-body", instead of a building housing a "kami-body". This type of mountain worship (''[[shintai-zan]]'') is found in the earliest forms of [[Shinto]], and has also been employed at [[Suwa Shrine]] in [[Nagano Prefecture|Nagano]], and formerly at [[Isonokami Shrine]] in [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] and [[Munakata Shrine]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]]. According to the chronicle ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', [[Emperor Sujin]] appealed to Mount Miwa's [[kami]] when Japan was crippled by [[Plague (disease)|plague]]. In response, the kami Ōmononushi demanded rituals be performed for him at Mount Miwa. He then demanded that the rites be led by [[Ōtata Neko]], his half-kami, half-human son born from the union with a woman of the [[Miwa clan]]. Ōtata Neko performed the rites to satisfaction, and the plague subsided. A building dedicated to Ōtata Neko was later erected in his honor. A legendary white snake is said to live in around the shrine, and is supposedly one of the kami worshipped there. Indeed, snakes and the snake cult figures importantly in the myths surrounding Mount Miwa as well as early Shinto in general. ==Architecture== [[Image:Ohmiwa Shrine 2nd Torii.jpg|thumb|170px|left|Second ''torii'' leading to the inner sanctuary]] Ōmiwa Shrine is situated in a quiet forest, and built directly in front of Mount Miwa. An ancient [[Japanese cedar]] tree (''Cryptomeria'') can be found on shrine compound, and is considered sacred. The mountain itself serves as the ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, instead of a man-made building. [[Image:AnneauxBorromeensFace.png|thumb|100px|right|Borromean rings motif]] Decorations in the form of [[Borromean rings]] are found throughout the shrine's buildings. This ornamentation symbolizes the three rings, as "Miwa" is written with the [[kanji]] for "three" (三) and "ring" (輪). Built in 1984, at 32 m the ''torii'' on its [[sandō]] is the second highest in Japan.{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/an/Bauten:Bekannte_Schreine|title= Bekannte Schreine - Religion-in-Japan|last=Scheid|first=Bernhard|publisher=University of Vienna|language=German|accessdate=20 September 2010}} The shrine also has a great ''shime torii'', an ancient form of gate made only with two posts and a rope called ''[[shimenawa]]''. It is one of few shrines that has a "triple-''torii''" (''[[miwa torii]]'') on its grounds. This gate is also one of the few to actually have doors, which bar access to the mountain it enshrines. The buildings at Ōmiwa Shrine are a mix of structures built from ancient times to the [[Edo period]]. ==National treasures== ===Important Cultural Assets=== *The entire shrine compound *The 17th century ''haiden'', or prayer hall, built with cypress [[bark]] roofing *The "Triple-''torii''" (''miwa torii'') *The ''shinden'' dedicated to [[Ōtata Neko]]. *Suit of bronze armor, lacquered red *A copy of the ''[[Book of Zhou]]'', scroll number 19. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Mount Miwa]] * [[Ko-shinto (Jomon)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[William George Aston|Aston, William George]]. He then demanded that the rites be led by [[Ōtata Neko]], his half-kami, half-human son born from the union with a woman of the [[Miwa clan]]. Ōtata Neko performed the rites to satisfaction, and the plague subsided. A building dedicated to Ōtata Neko was later erected in his honor. A legendary white snake is said to live in around the shrine, and is supposedly one of the kami worshipped there. Indeed, snakes and the snake cult figures importantly in the myths surrounding Mount Miwa as well as early Shinto in general. ===Auxiliiary shrines=== The Ōmiwa shrine complex includes notable auxiliary shrines (''[[setsumatsusha]]''), including 12 {{nihongo|''Sessha''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and 28 {{nihongo|''massha''|末社|branch shrine}} which are marked by small structures falling under Ōmiwa's jurisdiction.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 272-278. For example, the ''sessha'' ''Ikuhi jinja'' enshrines the ''kami'' who was appointed Ōmiwa's sake brewer in the 4th month of the 8th year of the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. A poem associated with Ikuhi is said to have been composed by [[Empress Jingu]] on the occasion of a banquet for her son, [[Emperor Ōjin]]:Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' p. 277. ::This is sacred sake :::is not my sacred sake. ::This sacred sake brewed by Ōmononushi ::How long ago ::How long ago. ==Architecture== [[Image:Ohmiwa Shrine 2nd Torii.jpg|thumb|170px|left|Second ''torii'' leading to the inner sanctuary]] Ōmiwa Shrine is situated in a quiet forest, and built directly in front of Mount Miwa. It is one of few shrines that has a "triple-''torii''" (''[[miwa torii]]'') on its grounds. This gate is also one of the few to actually have doors, which bar access to the mountain it enshrines. The buildings at Ōmiwa Shrine are a mix of structures built from ancient times to the [[Edo period]]. ==National treasures== ===Important Cultural Assets=== *The entire shrine compound *The 17th century ''haiden'', or prayer hall, built with cypress [[bark]] roofing Ponsonby-Fane, '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 269-271; note at p. 271, previous "''haiden'' built in [[Bumpo]] 1, and several times repaired by [[Ashikaga Takauji|Takauji]] and his descendants, and finally in [[Bunroku]] era by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Hideyoshi]] ... but no description of this building." *The "Triple-''torii''" (''miwa torii'') Ponsonby-Fane, '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' p. 271; note that "threefold torii take the place of the ''shinden'' of other shrines" *The ''shinden'' dedicated to [[Ōtata Neko]]. *Suit of bronze armor, lacquered red *A copy of the ''[[Book of Zhou]]'', scroll number 19. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Mount Miwa]] * [[Ko-shinto (Jomon)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[William George Aston|Aston, William George]]. [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] * {{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |location=Tokyo |year=2000 |chapter=The Birth of the Japanese Nation |isbn=4-333-01684-3 }} {{refend}} ==External links== {{commoncat|Ohmiwa-jinja|Ōmiwa Shrine}} *[http://www.oomiwa.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) {{coord|34|31|44|N|135|51|10|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Omiwa Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[ja:大神神社]]

{{about|the shrine in [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai city]]|similarly named shrines|Ōmiwa Shrine (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Ōmiwa jinja
大神神社 | image = Ohmiwa Shrine Big Torii.jpg | caption = Large ''[[torii]]'' in front of Mount Miwa | type = | dedication = [[Ōmononushi]]
[[Ōnamuchi]]
[[Sukunahikona-no-kami]]
[[Mount Miwa]] | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 1422 Miwa, [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai-shi]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara-ken]] | phone = 0744-42-6633 | website = http://www.oomiwa.or.jp/ }} {{nihongo|'''Ōmiwa jinja'''|大神神社|}}, also known as {{nihongo|'''Miwa Shrine'''|三輪神社|Miwa-jinja|alternately written as 三輪明神 ''Miwa-myōjin''}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai]], [[Nara prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 252-286. The shrine is noted because it contains no sacred images or objects because it is believed to serve [[Mount Miwa]], the mountain on which it stands.{{en icon}} [http://www.oomiwa.or.jp/eng.html Ōmiwa Shrine site] For the same reason, it has a {{nihongo|worship hall|拝殿|''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]''}}, but no {{nihongo|place for the deity to be housed|神殿|''shinden''}}. In this sense, it is a model of what the first Shinto shrines were like.Tamura, page 21 Ōmiwa Shrine is one of the oldest extant Shinto shrines in Japan and the site has been sacred ground for some of the [[Ko-shinto (Jomon)|earliest religious practices]] in Japan. Because of this, it has sometimes been named as Japan's first shrine. Ōmiwa Shrine is a tutelary shrine of the Japanese [[sake]] brewers. ==History== Ōmiwa Shrine's history is closely related to Mount Miwa and the religious practices surrounding the mountain. In the early [[Kofun period]], Yamato kings and leaders had shifted their attention to ''[[kami]]'' worship on Mount Miwa, and Ōmiwa Shrine was the major institution for this branch of worship.Brown (1993), 116-117. The style of Shinto surrounding Miwa became later known as [[Miwa Shinto]], and is set apart from previous practices by a more structured theological philosophy. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines, including Ōmiwa.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. Ōmiwa was designated as the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Yamato province]]. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Ōmiwa was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank among government supported shrines. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Religious significance== {{main|Mount Miwa}} The Ōmiwa Shrine is directly linked to Mount Miwa in that the mountain is the shrine's ''[[shintai]]'', or "kami-body", instead of a building housing a "kami-body". This type of mountain worship (''[[shintai-zan]]'') is found in the earliest forms of [[Shinto]], and has also been employed at [[Suwa Shrine]] in [[Nagano Prefecture|Nagano]], and formerly at [[Isonokami Shrine]] in [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] and [[Munakata Shrine]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]]. According to the chronicle ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', [[Emperor Sujin]] appealed to Mount Miwa's [[kami]] when Japan was crippled by [[Plague (disease)|plague]]. In response, the kami Ōmononushi demanded rituals be performed for him at Mount Miwa. He then demanded that the rites be led by [[Ōtata Neko]], his half-kami, half-human son born from the union with a woman of the [[Miwa clan]]. Ōtata Neko performed the rites to satisfaction, and the plague subsided. A building dedicated to Ōtata Neko was later erected in his honor. A legendary white snake is said to live in around the shrine, and is supposedly one of the kami worshipped there. Indeed, snakes and the snake cult figures importantly in the myths surrounding Mount Miwa as well as early Shinto in general. ===Auxiliiary shrines=== The Ōmiwa shrine complex includes notable auxiliary shrines (''[[setsumatsusha]]''), including 12 {{nihongo|''Sessha''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and 28 {{nihongo|''massha''|末社|branch shrine}} which are marked by small structures falling under Ōmiwa's jurisdiction.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 272-278. For example, the ''sessha'' ''Ikuhi jinja'' enshrines the ''kami'' who was appointed Ōmiwa's sake brewer in the 4th month of the 8th year of the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. A poem associated with Ikuhi is said to have been composed by [[Empress Jingu]] on the occasion of a banquet for her son, [[Emperor Ōjin]]:Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' p. 277. ::This is sacred sake :::is not my sacred sake. ::This sacred sake brewed by Ōmononushi ::How long ago ::How long ago. ==Architecture== [[Image:Ohmiwa Shrine 2nd Torii.jpg|thumb|170px|left|Second ''torii'' leading to the inner sanctuary]] Ōmiwa Shrine is situated in a quiet forest, and built directly in front of Mount Miwa. An ancient [[Japanese cedar]] tree (''Cryptomeria'') can be found on shrine compound, and is considered sacred. The mountain itself serves as the ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, instead of a man-made building. [[Image:AnneauxBorromeensFace.png|thumb|100px|right|Borromean rings motif]] Decorations in the form of [[Borromean rings]] are found throughout the shrine's buildings. This ornamentation symbolizes the three rings, as "Miwa" is written with the [[kanji]] for "three" (三) and "ring" (輪). Built in 1984, at 32 m the ''torii'' on its [[sandō]] is the second highest in Japan.{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/an/Bauten:Bekannte_Schreine|title= Bekannte Schreine - Religion-in-Japan|last=Scheid|first=Bernhard|publisher=University of Vienna|language=German|accessdate=20 September 2010}} The shrine also has a great ''shime torii'', an ancient form of gate made only with two posts and a rope called ''[[shimenawa]]''. It is one of few shrines that has a "triple-''torii''" (''[[miwa torii]]'') on its grounds. This gate is also one of the few to actually have doors, which bar access to the mountain it enshrines. The buildings at Ōmiwa Shrine are a mix of structures built from ancient times to the [[Edo period]]. ==National treasures== ===Important Cultural Assets=== *The entire shrine compound *The 17th century ''haiden'', or prayer hall, built with cypress [[bark]] roofing Ponsonby-Fane, '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 269-271; note at p. 271, previous "''haiden'' built in [[Bumpo]] 1, and several times repaired by [[Ashikaga Takauji|Takauji]] and his descendants, and finally in [[Bunroku]] era by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Hideyoshi]] ... but no description of this building." *The "Triple-''torii''" (''miwa torii'') Ponsonby-Fane, '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' p. 271; note that "threefold torii take the place of the ''shinden'' of other shrines" *The ''shinden'' dedicated to [[Ōtata Neko]]. *Suit of bronze armor, lacquered red *A copy of the ''[[Book of Zhou]]'', scroll number 19. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Mount Miwa]] * [[Ko-shinto (Jomon)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[William George Aston|Aston, William George]]. (2005). ''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697''. Boston: [[Tuttle Publishing]]. ISBN 0804836744. * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82363-4 * Brown, Delmer M. (1993). ''Cambridge History of Japan'', Volume 1. New York: [[Cambridge University Press]]. ISBN 0521223520. * Kidder, Jonathan Edward (2007). ''Himiko and Japan's elusive chiefdom of Yamatai: archaeology, history, and mythology''. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. ISBN 0824830350. * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [http://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&dq=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&client=firefox-a ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] * {{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |location=Tokyo |year=2000 |chapter=The Birth of the Japanese Nation |isbn=4-333-01684-3 }} {{refend}} ==External links== {{commoncat|Ohmiwa-jinja|Ōmiwa Shrine}} *[http://www.oomiwa.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) {{coord|34|31|44|N|135|51|10|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Omiwa Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[ja:大神神社]] [[zh:大神神社]]

''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Religious significance== {{main|Mount Miwa}} The Ōmiwa Shrine is directly linked to Mount Miwa in that the mountain is the shrine's ''[[shintai]]'', or "kami-body", instead of a building housing a "kami-body". This type of mountain worship (''[[shintai-zan]]'') is found in the earliest forms of [[Shinto]], and has also been employed at [[Suwa Shrine]] in [[Nagano Prefecture|Nagano]], and formerly at [[Isonokami Shrine]] in [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] and [[Munakata Shrine]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]]. According to the chronicle ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', [[Emperor Sujin]] appealed to Mount Miwa's [[kami]] when Japan was crippled by [[Plague (disease)|plague]]. In response, the kami Ōmononushi demanded rituals be performed for him at Mount Miwa. He then demanded that the rites be led by [[Ōtata Neko]], his half-kami, half-human son born from the union with a woman of the [[Miwa clan]]. Ōtata Neko performed the rites to satisfaction, and the plague subsided. A building dedicated to Ōtata Neko was later erected in his honor. A legendary white snake is said to live in around the shrine, and is supposedly one of the kami worshipped there. Indeed, snakes and the snake cult figures importantly in the myths surrounding Mount Miwa as well as early Shinto in general. ===Auxiliiary shrines=== The Ōmiwa shrine complex includes notable auxiliary shrines (''[[setsumatsusha]]''), including 12 {{nihongo|''Sessha''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and 28 {{nihongo|''massha''|末社|branch shrine}} which are marked by small structures falling under Ōmiwa's jurisdiction.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 272-278. For example, the ''sessha'' ''Ikuhi jinja'' enshrines the ''kami'' who was appointed Ōmiwa's sake brewer in the 4th month of the 8th year of the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. A poem associated with Ikuhi is said to have been composed by [[Empress Jingu]] on the occasion of a banquet for her son, [[Emperor Ōjin]]:Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' p. 277. ::This is sacred sake :::is not my sacred sake. ::This sacred sake brewed by Ōmononushi ::How long ago ::How long ago. ==Architecture== [[Image:Ohmiwa Shrine 2nd Torii.jpg|thumb|170px|left|Second ''torii'' leading to the inner sanctuary]] Ōmiwa Shrine is situated in a quiet forest, and built directly in front of Mount Miwa. An ancient [[Japanese cedar]] tree (''Cryptomeria'') can be found on shrine compound, and is considered sacred. The mountain itself serves as the ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, instead of a man-made building. [[Image:AnneauxBorromeensFace.png|thumb|100px|right|Borromean rings motif]] Decorations in the form of [[Borromean rings]] are found throughout the shrine's buildings. This ornamentation symbolizes the three rings, as "Miwa" is written with the [[kanji]] for "three" (三) and "ring" (輪). Built in 1984, at 32 m the ''torii'' on its [[sandō]] is the second highest in Japan.{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/an/Bauten:Bekannte_Schreine|title= Bekannte Schreine - Religion-in-Japan|last=Scheid|first=Bernhard|publisher=University of Vienna|language=German|accessdate=20 September 2010}} The shrine also has a great ''shime torii'', an ancient form of gate made only with two posts and a rope called ''[[shimenawa]]''. It is one of few shrines that has a "triple-''torii''" (''[[miwa torii]]'') on its grounds. This gate is also one of the few to actually have doors, which bar access to the mountain it enshrines. The buildings at Ōmiwa Shrine are a mix of structures built from ancient times to the [[Edo period]]. ==National treasures== ===Important Cultural Assets=== *The entire shrine compound *The 17th century ''haiden'', or prayer hall, built with cypress [[bark]] roofingPonsonby-Fane, '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 269-271; note at p. 271, previous "''haiden'' built in [[Bumpo]] 1, and several times repaired by [[Ashikaga Takauji|Takauji]] and his descendants, and finally in [[Bunroku]] era by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Hideyoshi]] ... but no description of this building." *The "Triple-''torii''" (''miwa torii'')Ponsonby-Fane, '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' p. 271; note that "threefold torii take the place of the ''shinden'' of other shrines" *The ''shinden'' dedicated to [[Ōtata Neko]]. *Suit of bronze armor, lacquered red *A copy of the ''[[Book of Zhou]]'', scroll number 19. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Mount Miwa]] * [[Ko-shinto (Jomon)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[William George Aston|Aston, William George]]. {{about|the shrine in [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai city]]|similarly named shrines|Ōmiwa Shrine (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Ōmiwa jinja
大神神社 | image = Ohmiwa Shrine Big Torii.jpg | caption = Large ''[[torii]]'' in front of Mount Miwa | type = | dedication = [[Ōmononushi]]
[[Ōnamuchi]]
[[Sukunahikona-no-kami]]
[[Mount Miwa]] | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 1422 Miwa, [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai-shi]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara-ken]] | phone = 0744-42-6633 | website = http://www.oomiwa.or.jp/ }} {{nihongo|'''Ōmiwa jinja'''|大神神社|}}, also known as {{nihongo|'''Miwa Shrine'''|三輪神社|Miwa-jinja|alternately written as 三輪明神 ''Miwa-myōjin''}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai]], [[Nara prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 252-286. The shrine is noted because it contains no sacred images or objects because it is believed to serve [[Mount Miwa]], the mountain on which it stands.{{en icon}} [http://www.oomiwa.or.jp/eng.html Ōmiwa Shrine site] For the same reason, it has a {{nihongo|worship hall|拝殿|''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]''}}, but no {{nihongo|place for the deity to be housed|神殿|''shinden''}}. In this sense, it is a model of what the first Shinto shrines were like.Tamura, page 21 Ōmiwa Shrine is one of the oldest extant Shinto shrines in Japan and the site has been sacred ground for some of the [[Ko-shinto (Jomon)|earliest religious practices]] in Japan. Because of this, it has sometimes been named as Japan's first shrine. Ōmiwa Shrine is a tutelary shrine of the Japanese [[sake]] brewers. ==History== Ōmiwa Shrine's history is closely related to Mount Miwa and the religious practices surrounding the mountain. In the early [[Kofun period]], Yamato kings and leaders had shifted their attention to ''[[kami]]'' worship on Mount Miwa, and Ōmiwa Shrine was the major institution for this branch of worship.Brown (1993), 116-117. The style of Shinto surrounding Miwa became later known as [[Miwa Shinto]], and is set apart from previous practices by a more structured theological philosophy. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines, including Ōmiwa.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. Ōmiwa was designated as the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Yamato province]]. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Ōmiwa was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank among government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Religious significance== {{main|Mount Miwa}} The Ōmiwa Shrine is directly linked to Mount Miwa in that the mountain is the shrine's ''[[shintai]]'', or "kami-body", instead of a building housing a "kami-body". In response, the kami Ōmononushi demanded rituals be performed for him at Mount Miwa. He then demanded that the rites be led by [[Ōtata Neko]], his half-kami, half-human son born from the union with a woman of the [[Miwa clan]]. Ōtata Neko performed the rites to satisfaction, and the plague subsided. A building dedicated to Ōtata Neko was later erected in his honor. A legendary white snake is said to live in around the shrine, and is supposedly one of the kami worshipped there. Indeed, snakes and the snake cult figures importantly in the myths surrounding Mount Miwa as well as early Shinto in general. ===Auxiliiary shrines=== The Ōmiwa shrine complex includes notable auxiliary shrines (''[[setsumatsusha]]''), including 12 {{nihongo|''Sessha''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and 28 {{nihongo|''massha''|末社|branch shrine}} which are marked by small structures falling under Ōmiwa's jurisdiction.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 272-278. For example, the ''sessha'' ''Ikuhi jinja'' enshrines the ''kami'' who was appointed Ōmiwa's sake brewer in the 4th month of the 8th year of the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. A poem associated with Ikuhi is said to have been composed by [[Empress Jingu]] on the occasion of a banquet for her son, [[Emperor Ōjin]]:Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' p. 277. ::This is sacred sake :::is not my sacred sake. ::This sacred sake brewed by Ōmononushi ::How long ago ::How long ago. ==Architecture== [[Image:Ohmiwa Shrine 2nd Torii.jpg|thumb|170px|left|Second ''torii'' leading to the inner sanctuary]] Ōmiwa Shrine is situated in a quiet forest, and built directly in front of Mount Miwa. The buildings at Ōmiwa Shrine are a mix of structures built from ancient times to the [[Edo period]]. ==National treasures== ===Important Cultural Assets=== *The entire shrine compound *The 17th century ''haiden'', or prayer hall, built with cypress [[bark]] roofingPonsonby-Fane, '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 269-271; note at p. 271, previous "''haiden'' built in [[Bumpo]] 1, and several times repaired by [[Ashikaga Takauji|Takauji]] and his descendants, and finally in [[Bunroku]] era by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Hideyoshi]] ... but no description of this building." *The "Triple-''torii''" (''miwa torii'')Ponsonby-Fane, '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' p. 271; note that "threefold torii take the place of the ''shinden'' of other shrines" *The ''shinden'' dedicated to [[Ōtata Neko]]. *Suit of bronze armor, lacquered red *A copy of the ''[[Book of Zhou]]'', scroll number 19. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Mount Miwa]] * [[Ko-shinto (Jomon)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[William George Aston|Aston, William George]]. {{about|the shrine in [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai city]]|similarly named shrines|Ōmiwa Shrine (disambiguation)}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Ōmiwa jinja
大神神社 | image = Ohmiwa Shrine Big Torii.jpg | caption = Large ''[[torii]]'' in front of Mount Miwa | type = | dedication = [[Ōmononushi]]
[[Ōnamuchi]]
[[Sukunahikona-no-kami]]
[[Mount Miwa]] | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 1422 Miwa, [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai-shi]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara-ken]] | phone = 0744-42-6633 | website = http://www.oomiwa.or.jp/ }} {{nihongo|'''Ōmiwa jinja'''|大神神社|}}, also known as {{nihongo|'''Miwa Shrine'''|三輪神社|Miwa-jinja|alternately written as 三輪明神 ''Miwa-myōjin''}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai]], [[Nara prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 252-286. The shrine is noted because it contains no sacred images or objects because it is believed to serve [[Mount Miwa]], the mountain on which it stands.{{en icon}} [http://www.oomiwa.or.jp/eng.html Ōmiwa Shrine site] For the same reason, it has a {{nihongo|worship hall|拝殿|''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]''}}, but no {{nihongo|place for the deity to be housed|神殿|''shinden''}}. In this sense, it is a model of what the first Shinto shrines were like.Tamura, page 21 Ōmiwa Shrine is one of the oldest extant Shinto shrines in Japan and the site has been sacred ground for some of the [[Ko-shinto (Jomon)|earliest religious practices]] in Japan. Because of this, it has sometimes been named as Japan's first shrine. Ōmiwa Shrine is a tutelary shrine of the Japanese [[sake]] brewers. ==History== Ōmiwa Shrine's history is closely related to Mount Miwa and the religious practices surrounding the mountain. In the early [[Kofun period]], Yamato kings and leaders had shifted their attention to ''[[kami]]'' worship on Mount Miwa, and Ōmiwa Shrine was the major institution for this branch of worship.Brown (1993), 116-117. The style of Shinto surrounding Miwa became later known as [[Miwa Shinto]], and is set apart from previous practices by a more structured theological philosophy. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines, including Ōmiwa.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. Ōmiwa was designated as the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Yamato province]]. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 From 1871 through 1946, Ōmiwa was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank among government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Religious significance== {{main|Mount Miwa}} The Ōmiwa Shrine is directly linked to Mount Miwa in that the mountain is the shrine's ''[[shintai]]'', or "kami-body", instead of a building housing a "kami-body". This type of mountain worship (''[[shintai-zan]]'') is found in the earliest forms of [[Shinto]], and has also been employed at [[Suwa Shrine]] in [[Nagano Prefecture|Nagano]], and formerly at [[Isonokami Shrine]] in [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] and [[Munakata Shrine]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]]. According to the chronicle ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', [[Emperor Sujin]] appealed to Mount Miwa's [[kami]] when Japan was crippled by [[Plague (disease)|plague]]. In response, the kami Ōmononushi demanded rituals be performed for him at Mount Miwa. He then demanded that the rites be led by [[Ōtata Neko]], his half-kami, half-human son born from the union with a woman of the [[Miwa clan]]. Ōtata Neko performed the rites to satisfaction, and the plague subsided. A building dedicated to Ōtata Neko was later erected in his honor. A legendary white snake is said to live in around the shrine, and is supposedly one of the kami worshipped there. Indeed, snakes and the snake cult figures importantly in the myths surrounding Mount Miwa as well as early Shinto in general. ===Auxiliiary shrines=== The Ōmiwa shrine complex includes notable auxiliary shrines (''[[setsumatsusha]]''), including 12 {{nihongo|''Sessha''|摂社|auxiliary shrine}} and 28 {{nihongo|''massha''|末社|branch shrine}} which are marked by small structures falling under Ōmiwa's jurisdiction.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 272-278. For example, the ''sessha'' ''Ikuhi jinja'' enshrines the ''kami'' who was appointed Ōmiwa's sake brewer in the 4th month of the 8th year of the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]]. A poem associated with Ikuhi is said to have been composed by [[Empress Jingu]] on the occasion of a banquet for her son, [[Emperor Ōjin]]:Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' p. 277. ::This is sacred sake :::is not my sacred sake. ::This sacred sake brewed by Ōmononushi ::How long ago ::How long ago. ==Architecture== [[File:Ohmiwa Shrine 2nd Torii.jpg|thumb|170px|left|Second ''torii'' leading to the inner sanctuary]] Ōmiwa Shrine is situated in a quiet forest, and built directly in front of Mount Miwa. An ancient [[Japanese cedar]] tree (''Cryptomeria'') can be found on shrine compound, and is considered sacred. The mountain itself serves as the ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, instead of a man-made building. [[File:AnneauxBorromeensFace.png|thumb|100px|right|Borromean rings motif]] Decorations in the form of [[Borromean rings]] are found throughout the shrine's buildings. This ornamentation symbolizes the three rings, as "Miwa" is written with the [[kanji]] for "three" (三) and "ring" (輪). Built in 1984, at 32 m the ''torii'' on its [[sandō]] is the second highest in Japan.{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/an/Bauten:Bekannte_Schreine|title= Bekannte Schreine - Religion-in-Japan|last=Scheid|first=Bernhard|publisher=University of Vienna|language=German|accessdate=20 September 2010}} The shrine also has a great ''shime torii'', an ancient form of gate made only with two posts and a rope called ''[[shimenawa]]''. It is one of few shrines that has a "triple-''torii''" (''[[miwa torii]]'') on its grounds. This gate is also one of the few to actually have doors, which bar access to the mountain it enshrines. The buildings at Ōmiwa Shrine are a mix of structures built from ancient times to the [[Edo period]]. ==National treasures== ===Important Cultural Assets=== *The entire shrine compound *The 17th century ''haiden'', or prayer hall, built with cypress [[bark]] roofingPonsonby-Fane, '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 269-271; note at p. 271, previous "''haiden'' built in [[Bumpo]] 1, and several times repaired by [[Ashikaga Takauji|Takauji]] and his descendants, and finally in [[Bunroku]] era by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Hideyoshi]] ... but no description of this building." *The "Triple-''torii''" (''miwa torii'')Ponsonby-Fane, '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' p. 271; note that "threefold torii take the place of the ''shinden'' of other shrines" *The ''shinden'' dedicated to [[Ōtata Neko]]. *Suit of bronze armor, lacquered red *A copy of the ''[[Book of Zhou]]'', scroll number 19. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Mount Miwa]] * [[Ko-shinto (Jomon)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[William George Aston|Aston, William George]]. (2005). ''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697''. Boston: [[Tuttle Publishing]]. ISBN 0-8048-3674-4. * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-8248-2363-4 * Brown, Delmer M. (1993). ''Cambridge History of Japan'', Volume 1. New York: [[Cambridge University Press]]. ISBN 0-521-22352-0. * Kidder, Jonathan Edward (2007). ''Himiko and Japan's elusive chiefdom of Yamatai: archaeology, history, and mythology''. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. ISBN 0-8248-3035-0. * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [http://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&dq=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&client=firefox-a ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] * {{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism - A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |location=Tokyo |year=2000 |chapter=The Birth of the Japanese Nation |isbn=4-333-01684-3 }} {{refend}} ==External links== {{commons category|Ōmiwa-jinja|Ōmiwa jinja}} *[http://www.oomiwa.or.jp/ Official Site] (Japanese) *[http://www.oomiwa.or.jp/eng.html Official Site] (English) {{coord|34|31|44|N|135|51|10|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Omiwa Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[ja:大神神社]] [[zh:大神神社]]
